name: Hyattville
short_description: BLM rockhounding near the Big Horn Mountains. Search for agate, jasper, and chalcedony on public land with no permit required and generous 25 lb daily limits.
description:
Hyattville sits on productive BLM land in the northern Big Horn Basin, one of Wyoming's premier rockhounding regions. The moderate-slope terrain at 5,046 feet elevation is accessible via good roads and offers excellent exposed mineral deposits in washes and hillsides. This area is known for quality agate and jasper specimens that weather out from Tertiary volcanic and sedimentary formations.

The geology here reflects Eocene to Miocene volcanic activity and deposition across the Bighorn Basin. Silica-rich fluids percolated through existing sediments and created nodular agate, layered jasper, and chalcedony in fractures and veins. Over millions of years, erosion exposed these gemstones at the surface where rockhounders can find them without digging.

The landscape is semi-arid high desert with scattered sage, grasses, and low shrubs. Washes and drainage cuts provide the best collecting spots, where weathering continuously exposes fresh material. The area sees moderate visitation and offers good solitude compared to more famous Wyoming sites.

Bring plenty of water, sturdy boots, and a small pry bar or rock hammer. The rocky footing is uneven but not extreme. This site works well for collectors of all skill levels who respect BLM regulations and leave the land in good condition.
history: The Bighorn Basin sits within the Laramide structural belt, shaped by major uplift events 80-55 million years ago. Tertiary volcanic ash falls and subsequent silica precipitation created ideal conditions for agate and jasper formation in this region. Historical prospectors worked the basin throughout the 20th century, and today's rockhounders benefit from well-established collecting areas with predictable gem deposits.
tips: Search washes and eroded hillsides where water has exposed fresh agate and jasper nodules; the exposed gravels and sandy banks are your best bet. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and hand tools (chisel or pry bar) to work around specimens; motorized equipment is prohibited on BLM land. Look for translucent banding and warm colors (reds, oranges, browns, whites) in the rocks you find; avoid collecting large rocks unless they are clearly gem-quality specimens to stay within the 25 lb daily limit. Early morning rockhounding in spring and fall avoids midday heat and gives you better light to spot colorful specimens.
terrain_notes: Elevation 5,046 feet with moderate rolling slopes. Semi-arid high desert vegetation including sagebrush and grasses. Rocky, uneven footing with exposed bedrock and talus. Walking distance to productive areas typically under 1 mile from parking. No wheelchair access. Terrain is stable but requires sturdy boots and careful foot placement.
best_season: May - October. The high elevation means winters are harsh and spring runoff affects road conditions. Summer (June - August) is warm but manageable. Fall (September - October) offers ideal conditions with cooler temperatures and low precipitation. Avoid November - April when snow and cold weather limit access and visibility.
written_directions: From Sheridan, Wyoming, take WY-14 west toward Big Horn for approximately 15 miles. Continue through Big Horn and follow county roads south and west toward the Hyattville area. BLM collecting areas are accessed via pullouts and ranch roads on the western flank of the Big Horn Mountains. Use GPS coordinates 44.244134, -107.525982 to locate the main collecting zone. Road surface is graded gravel; high-clearance vehicle recommended after heavy rain.
road_conditions: Graded gravel and dirt roads in good condition. High-clearance vehicle recommended after rain. No pavement required for main access areas.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts and gravel turnarounds along access roads. No formal parking lot. Space for 2-4 vehicles at most pullouts. Park off the traveled surface.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or equipment. No collection of fossils or cultural artifacts. Remove only what you intend to keep; do not damage rock outcrops. Pack out all trash. Respect all private land boundaries marked on maps.
rules:

Respect private land boundaries
nearest_services: Sheridan, Wyoming approximately 25 miles north. Full services including gas, food, water, lodging, and supply stores. Big Horn village (10 miles north) has limited services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to rolling terrain accessible to most hikers with sturdy boots. Uneven rocky footing and no wheelchair access. Most productive collecting areas are within easy walking distance (under 1 mile) of pullouts. Suitable for families with children ages 6 and older. Limited shade and exposure to sun and wind.

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding near Hyattville, Wyoming?
faq.answer: Hyattville BLM land is known for quality agate, jasper, chalcedony, and quartz specimens. The agate often displays warm reds, oranges, and browns with translucent banding. Jasper here tends toward red and picture varieties. Most specimens are nodules or small stones found weathering out of washes and eroded hillsides.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Hyattville on BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Hyattville. It is open to public collecting under BLM personal-use regulations. You are limited to 25 lbs per day, and collection must be for personal use only - no commercial collecting allowed.

faq.question: What is the best time to go rockhounding near Hyattville, Wyoming?
faq.answer: The best seasons are May through October. Spring (May - June) offers cool temperatures and active water flow in washes that exposes fresh material. Fall (September - October) provides ideal conditions with mild weather. Avoid winter months (November - April) when snow and cold make access difficult at this 5,046 ft elevation.

faq.question: How far is Hyattville rockhounding from the nearest town with supplies?
faq.answer: Sheridan, Wyoming is approximately 25 miles north of Hyattville and offers all services including gas, food, lodging, and rockhounding supplies. The smaller town of Big Horn is about 10 miles north with limited services. Plan to fuel and supply up before heading to remote BLM collecting areas.

faq.question: Is Hyattville a good rockhounding site for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Hyattville BLM land is beginner and family friendly. Specimens are found on or near the surface with minimal digging required. The terrain is moderate and walking distances are short. Children ages 6 and up can participate. No special tools are needed, though a pry bar or rock hammer helps. The 25 lb daily limit and easy access make it perfect for learning.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need for rockhounding at Hyattville?
faq.answer: Bring a small pry bar, rock hammer, or chisel to work around specimens. A 5-gallon bucket helps carry finds within the 25 lb daily limit. Wear sturdy boots for rocky terrain. Pack 2-3 gallons of water, sun protection, and a map showing BLM boundaries and private land. A loupe or hand lens helps identify gem quality. Avoid motorized equipment, which is prohibited.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Hyattville, Wyoming. Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony. No permit needed, 25 lb daily limit. Perfect for families and beginners.
